coredata.dc:title: Solar spectrum photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CH4 utilizing TiO2 nanotube arrays embedded with graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zubair, Muhammad
Kim, HyeRim
Razzaq, Abdul
Grimes, Craig A.
In, Su-Il
coredata.dc:description: TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNT) offer an exciting prospect as a photocatalytic material architecture due to the combined properties of high surface area, 1-D vectorial charge transfer, and reduced photogenerated charge recombination. However the TiO2 band gap (≈3.2 eV) limits light absorption to the UV region, which comprises but a small fraction of the solar spectrum energy. Graphene is known to effectively absorb visible light, and due to its high conductivity promote efficient charge transfer. Herein, we present a novel photocatalyst composed of TNTs sensitized with electrodeposited graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs electrodeposition-duration is varied to optimize photocatalytic performance of the resulting nanostructured graphene-TNT (G-TNT) films. Under solar spectrum illumination we find optimal G-TNT samples promote a CO2 to CH4 photocatalytic conversion rate of 1.98 ppm cm−2 h−1, with carbon origin confirmed by 13CO2 isotopic test.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanotubes
Graphene quantum dots
Nanostructure
Photocatalytic CO2 conversion
Methylene Blue dye degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CH4

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful for the funding support by the DGIST R&D Program of the Ministry of Science and ICT ( 18-BD-0404 ) and Basic Science research program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( 2017R1E1A1A01074890 ). Authors also acknowledge the support by the Technology Development Program to Solve Climate Changes of the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( 2015M1A2A2074670 ). Authors are also thankful to the colleagues and friends for their kind support and help. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Energy level diagram for photocatalytic CO2 conversion into CH4 using graphene quantum-dot sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays.
Schematic illustration of synthesis procedure for GQDs-sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays (G-TNT) samples.
FE-SEM images showing the surface morphology and cross-section of a TNT (a & c) and G-TNT3 sample (b & d).
TEM images of (a) GQDs and (c) G-TNT3 sample. HR-TEM images of (b) GQDs and (d) G-TNT3 sample with lattice spacing.
XRD patterns of: (a) TNT, (b) G-TNT1, (c) G-TNT2, (d) G-TNT3, (e) G-TNT4, and (f) G-TNT5 samples.
Raman spectra of: (a) GQDs, TNT, and G-TNT samples; and (b) enlarged view of D and G bands for respective samples.
High-resolution XPS analysis of: (a) Ti 2p and (b) O 1s for TNT and G-TNT3 samples. Deconvoluted XPS peaks for sample G-TNT3 showing (c) O 1s and (d) C 1s regions.
UV–vis DRS of TNT and G-TNT samples.
Transient photocurrent response of TNT and G-TNT samples under AM 1.5 illumination.
Rate constant measurements (a,b) for MB dye degradation; (c) photocatalytic stability of sample G-TNT3 for MB dye degradation; (d) proposed mechanism for photocatalytic MB dye degradation.

(a) Photocatalytic CH4 formation rates by TNT and G-TNT samples under AM 1.5 illumination; (b) stability test for photocatalytic CO2 conversion employing the same G-TNT3 sample for five consecutive cycles (without intermediate thermo-vacuum treatment); (c) stability test employing the same G-TNT3 sample for five consecutive cycles with intermediate thermo-vacuum treatment.

head.ce:title: Solar spectrum photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CH4 utilizing TiO2 nanotube arrays embedded with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Science and Engineering, DGIST, 333 Techno Jungang-daero, Hyeonpung-myeon, Dalseong-gun, Daegu, 42988, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, COMSATS Institute of Information Technology, 1.5 KM Defence Road, Off Raiwind Road, Lahore, 54000, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Flux Photon Corporation, 6900 Darcy Lane, Raleigh, NC, 27606, United States

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNT) offer an exciting prospect as a photocatalytic material architecture due to the combined properties of high surface area, 1-D vectorial charge transfer, and reduced photogenerated charge recombination. However the TiO2 band gap (≈3.2 eV) limits light absorption to the UV region, which comprises but a small fraction of the solar spectrum energy. Graphene is known to effectively absorb visible light, and due to its high conductivity promote efficient charge transfer. Herein, we present a novel photocatalyst composed of TNTs sensitized with electrodeposited graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs electrodeposition-duration is varied to optimize photocatalytic performance of the resulting nanostructured graphene-TNT (G-TNT) films. Under solar spectrum illumination we find optimal G-TNT samples promote a CO2 to CH4 photocatalytic conversion rate of 1.98 ppm cm−2 h−1, with carbon origin confirmed by 13CO2 isotopic test.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been extensively investigated for a variety of applications including gas sensing [1], dye-sensitized solar cells [2,3], photocatalytic organic pollutant degradation [4,5], self-cleaning surfaces [6], superhydrophilic surfaces [7], hydrogen production by water splitting [8–10], and photocatalytic conversion of CO2 into fuel [11–13]. Despite the appealing properties of TiO2, such as low cost, nontoxicity, excellent thermal and mechanical stability, the photocatalytic utility of TiO2 is largely limited to the UV region by its relatively large band gap of approximately 3.2 eV.

There have been many studies aimed towards shifting the optical response of TiO2 towards the visible spectrum, which contains the majority of the solar spectrum energy. Such studies have included the use of TiO2 doping [14–17], heterojunctioned TiO2-based materials [18–20], and nanostructured TiO2 materials [13,21,22]. Among the many nanostructured TiO2 materials, well-ordered TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNT), grown from a Titanium (Ti) substrate by electrochemical anodization [23] has been an area of considerable interest with the material architecture providing a large surface area, directional charge transfer, and a rate of photogenerated charge recombination significantly lower than that of TiO2 nanoparticles [24,25].

Further, while TiO2 is intrinsically stable, doped and heterojunctioned TiO2 face challenges of relatively poor long-term material stability [26]. Therefore, an effective approach is still needed for the synthesis of TiO2 and/or TiO2 based materials with significantly enhanced visible-spectrum photocatalytic properties. During the last decade, graphene and graphene derivatives have gained considerable interest due to their unique optical and electronic properties [27]. The hypothesis motivating our work is that a high-performance, stable, visible-light responsive photocatalyst can be achieved by sensitizing TiO2 nanotube arrays with graphene or graphene-derivatives. In this regard, different researchers have electrochemically deposited graphene on TNT and applied them for different photocatalytic applications [28,29]. Ming and co-workers [29], for example, electrochemically deposited reduced graphene oxide (rGO) onto TNT films, investigating photoelectrochemical properties by monitoring the photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange. Bipin et al. [30] synthesized TNT films, filled their pores with GQDs, and investigated the utility of the material for photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) under UV light. In et al. [31] synthesized TNT films sensitized with rGO-TiO2 nanoparticles utilizing simultaneous electrophoretic deposition and reduction of GO onto Ti foil, with subsequent electrochemical anodization of this rGO-Ti substrate. These studies found enhanced photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic properties, which leads to our hypothesis that an enhanced graphene deposition technique that provides greater material uniformity should lead to superior activity in regard to photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to fuel, as depicted in Fig. 1 .

Herein, we report synthesis and application of GQDs electrodeposited upon TNT substrates (G-TNT). It was expected that the GQDs would enhance photocatalytic performance due to the demonstrated ability of graphene to capture electrons, thus decreasing the recombination rate of TiO2 photogenerated charge carriers [32]. A schematic illustration of the synthesis approach is shown in Fig. 2 . The performance of the G-TNT films is investigated by employing them for photocatalytic conversion of CO2 and water vapor into CH4 under simulated solar light, as well as methylene blue (MB) dye degradation.

Citric acid (CA, ACS reagent, ≥99.5%) and methylene blue (MB) dye were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethylene glycol (EG, extra pure) and ammonium fluoride (NH4F, ACS reagent, 98%) were purchased from Daejung (Republic of Korea) and Alfa Aesar respectively. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 94%) and concentrated sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 95%) were procured by Duksan reagents (Republic of Korea). Titanium foil (Ti, 0.1 mm thickness, 99.5%) were ordered from Nilaco Corporation (Japan), and deionized (DI) water was used throughout the entire study. Sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, 99%), used as an electrolyte for photoelectrochemical studies, was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents and chemicals were used as received without further modification.

GQDs were synthesized as previously reported [33]. Briefly, 2 g of citric acid was placed in a glass vial (5 ml) raised in temperature, via a heating mantle, to 300 °C by which the citric acid turned orange. This was then added to 100 ml of NaOH solution (10 mg ml−1) under vigorous stirring, followed by sonication for 10 min. The pH of the solution was neutralized by adding a few drops of concentrated H2SO4, finally resulting in an aqueous solution of GQDs.

G-TNT samples were fabricated by a two-step electrochemical anodization method. Ti foils (2 cm × 2 cm) were sequentially washed with acetone, ethanol and DI water, in each step being sonicated for 10 min, dried using a N2 gun, then anodized in a two-electrode assembly consisting of Ti foil as the working electrode (anode) and carbon paper as a counter electrode (cathode). Anodization was performed in a Teflon beaker (100 ml) containing an electrolyte composed of 0.25 weight percent (wt.%) NH4F and 2.0 volume percent (vol.%) DI water in 100 ml ethylene glycol (EG). The first anodization was carried out for 15 min at a potential of 50 V, with an electrode spacing of 2.5 cm. The resulting layer of TiO2 nanotubes was then removed by sonication in DI water, leaving the Ti foil substrate with a highly-ordered dimpled morphology. The GQDs were then electrophoretically deposited onto this substrate [31]; briefly, the Ti foil substrate served as the anode and a stainless steel (2 cm × 2 cm) mesh as the cathode, 0.5 cm spacing, immersed into 50 ml of the GQDs solution; a bias of 10 V was applied. Various samples were prepared by changing the time duration of the electrodeposition voltage applied, i.e. 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 s. After electrodeposition of the GQDs the sample was rinsed with DI water, air dried, and then subjected to a second anodization. The second anodization step utilized a fresh electrolyte of the same composition, but a voltage of 50 V applied for 30 min. Finally, then, the G-TNT samples were rinsed with DI water, air dried, and annealed under Ar atmosphere for 1 h at 400 °C with a ramp of 2 °C min−1. The G-TNT samples were named G-TNT1, G-TNT2, G-TNT3, G-TNT4, and G-TNT5, respectively denoting 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 s GQDs deposition duration. A TNT sample, without GQDs sensitization, was synthesized by identical electrochemical anodization steps and used as a reference sample.

Sample crystallinity was investigated using an X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical, Empyrean) operating at 40 kV and 30 mA with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were taken in the range of 2θ = 15–80° with a step time of 0.5 s and step degree of 0.02. Morphological studies were done using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI/Czech) operating at 200 kV. TEM samples were prepared by placing one drop of GQDs or dispersed G-TNT (in methanol) on a lacey carbon copper (Cu) grid which was then dried at ambient temperature for 12 h. The G-TNT TEM sample was prepared by scratching the surface of a planar G-TNT sample and dispersing the collected powder, in methanol, by mild sonication (5 min). Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was utilized for elemental mapping of G-TNT samples. Raman spectra were obtained using a Nicolet Almeca XR, (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) having a 532 nm He-Ne laser light source. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were measured using a Cary series UV–vis-near IR spectrophotometer. Surface analysis and element oxidization states were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, thermo VG, Kα) equipped with Al Kα line (148,606 eV) X-ray source.

Photocurrent and electrical impedance measurements under dark and solar simulated light illumination were measured using a potentiostat (Bio-Logics, VSP model, France). Photocurrent and electrical impendence measurements were performed using a three-electrode configuration, with the synthesized sample used as the working electrode with area of 1.0 cm2, platinum (Pt) wire as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode, all immersed in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution. A 100 W Xenon solar simulator (Oriel, LCS-100) with an AM 1.5 filter was used to illuminate the electrochemical cell. The photocurrent was also measured under chopped illumination, with an on/off interval of 20 s, while applying a bias of 0.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Impedance was measured over the frequency range of 1 MHz–1 Hz in both light and dark.

Photocatalytic degradation of MB dye by TNT and G-TNT samples was investigated under simulated light at ambient temperature. The 2 cm × 2 cm photocatalyst samples were vertically dipped in MB dye solution (0.04 mmol L−1) and stirred under dark for 30 min to obtain adsorption-desorption equilibrium, whereupon the system was illuminated, during which 3.0 ml of the dye solution was taken from the beaker after every 30 min, with dye degradation analyzed by monitoring the change in MB dye concentration at its typical absorption wavelength (664 nm) using a Shimadzu UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer [34]. The stability of sample G-TNT3 was investigated by subjecting the sample to four MB dye degradation cycles; after every cycle the sample was washed with DI water and dried in a vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CH4
ce:section.ce:para: A gas phase reactor was utilized as reported previously [35]; a schematic illustration of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. S1. Briefly, a stainless steel photoreactor (15.4 cm3) with quartz window was loaded with a (2 cm × 2 cm) sample. The photoreactor was then purged with CO2 gas (1000 ppm in He) followed by vacuum, the cycle repeated three times to remove impurity gases. The photoreactor was then filled with a CO2 gas passing through a water bubbler. The photocatalytic CO2 reduction experiments were conducted under three different light sources: (1) A 100 W Xenon solar simulator (Oriel, LCS-100) with an AM 1.5 filter to provide 1 sun condition; (2) a 100 W Xenon solar simulator in combination with a 430 nm cut-off (λ > 430 nm) filter; (3) a UV lamp (UVGL‐58, λ = 365 nm, 1.2 mW cm−2). After 1 h continuous illumination, a 500 μL sample was removed using an argon purged glass syringe and manually injected into a gas chromatography unit (Shimadzu GC-2014, Restek RT-Q-Bond column, ID = 0.53 mm, length = 30 m) equipped with flame ionization detector (FID).

The time-normalized photocatalytic CH4 evolution rate is calculated:
(1)

4
e v o l u t i o n

4

( p p m )
E x p o s e d

o f
p h o t o c a t a l y s t

2
× t i m e
( h )

To investigate the origin of carbon inherent to CH4 formation, a G-TNT3 sample was loaded into the photoreactor, the chamber filled with an Ar/H2O(g) mixture, and illuminated under similar conditions with products GC analyzed. A 13CO2 isotopic test was done under similar illumination conditions. 13CO2 isotopic gas diluted up to concentration of 500 ppm in He by using 13CO2 (99%, Aldrich). The gas was allowed to pass through the water bubbler to obtain a 13CO2/H2O(g) mixture, thereupon passing into the He purged photoreactor loaded with a G-TNT3 sample; after 3 h illumination, the resulting products were analyzed by a gas chromatography–mass spectroscopy unit (GC–MS, Shimadzu, GCMS-QP2010 model, Restek Q-Bond column, ID = 0.32 mm, length = 30 m). Photocatalytic stability was investigated by employing the same photocatalyst sample for five consecutive CO2 conversion cycles.

The surface image of a clean Ti foil (Fig. S2a) exhibits a rough surface, while after the 1st anodization step (Fig. S2b) the surface displays growth of random and non-uniform TiO2 nanotube arrays. Fig. S2c shows a more uniform Ti substrate surface as compared to pure Ti foil with more smoothness and uniformity after removal of TiO2 nanotubes grown during first anodization via sonication, whereas Fig. S2d depicts a GQDs-electrodeposited Ti substrate utilized for the 2nd anodization step. Applying a positive potential to the Ti substrate, immersed in GQDs solution, attracts the negatively charged GQDs [31,36,37]. The surface image of a GQDs-electrodeposited TiO2 nanotubes, representative sample G-TNT3, obtained after the 2nd electrochemical anodization process is shown in Fig. S2e, displaying uniform, clean, well-oriented TNTs with a hierarchical nano-structure.

It appears that when the GQDs-deposited Ti substrate (G-Ti) is electrochemically anodized the positive bias pushes Ti4+ ions to the outer surface where they interact with OH− and F− ions in the electrolyte, forming soluble complexes [37] while promoting nanotube growth. It is presumed that the nanotubes grow upon the initial pattern created in the first anodization step due to its lower electric resistance (thinner metal layer), leaving behind GQDs on the circumference of the nanotubes. The G-TNT samples thus formed are subsequently annealed under an Ar environment for improved crystallinity.

Fig. 3 shows surface and cross-sectional FE-SEM images of a TNT and G-TNT3 sample; both show clean, uniform, and open pores, known characteristics of TNT films (Fig. 3a & b). The cross-sectional image of the TNT sample (Fig. 3c) shows a nanotube length of 4.15–4.18 μm, whereas the G-TNT3 displays a length of 3.81–3.89 μm (Fig. 3d). GQDs presence is difficult to observe by FE-SEM due to their small size.

Fig. 4 shows transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of pure GQDs and a G-TNT3 sample. The size of the as-synthesized GQDs is ≈2 nm (Fig. 4a), with respective lattice fringes of 0.35 nm corresponding to d002 plane of GQDs (Fig. 3b) [38–40]. The HR-TEM image of sample G-TNT3 (Fig. 4d) also exhibits an interplanar spacing of 0.35 nm, presumably due to the d101 plane of anatase TiO2 [41,42]. To confirm the presence of GQDs within sample G-TNT3, elemental mapping was performed by Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), see Fig. S3; the presence of carbon, along with titanium and oxygen, can be seen within the selected portion of the nanotube surface.

Fig. 5 shows the XRD patterns for TNT and various G-TNT samples. All samples exhibit peaks associated with anatase (A) TiO2 along with minor peaks of titanium (Ti). Further, XRD peaks for GQDs are not observed in any G-TNT sample, presumably due to the low graphene content [29]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of GQDs (Fig. S4) exhibit a broad peak appearing around 2θ = 25.4°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphene with a lattice fringe of 0.35 nm, the same value as observed in TEM analysis [27].

Raman spectroscopy was used to characterize GQDs, TNT, and G-TNT samples, see Fig. 6 . It can be observed that the as-synthesized GQDs sample shows prominent raman peaks around 1358 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 ascribed, respectively, to the D and G bands. As is well known, the raman D and G bands are the main features for investigating graphene structure [43] with the G band expressing sp2 hybrid carbon material and the D band providing information about the sp3 defects in hexagonal graphitic structure [29]. Raman spectra for the G-TNT samples also exhibit the D and G bands, which are not observed in the TNT samples. However, the intensity ratio of the D to G bands (ID/IG) for the G-TNT samples is low as compared to that of pure GQDs, a behavior that can be attributed to formation of but a few high quality graphene layers [44,45]. The decreased D band intensity, with a blue shift from 1358 cm−1 to 1425 cm−1 in the G-TNT samples indicates the presence of high quality GQDs upon the surface [27]. While the exact reason for lower D band intensity, and its blue-shifting, is not clearly understood we suggest that during electrochemical anodization of Ti foil the interaction of the Ti4+ ions with the electrodeposited GQDs results in a decrease of the sp3 carbon defect signature, and thus decreased D band intensity [31,46]. Raman spectra for the TNT samples show only peaks associated with anatase, i.e. those appearing around 148 cm−1, 400 cm−1, 515 cm−1 and 640 cm−1, respectively ascribed to Eg, B1g, A1g and Eg modes [47,48].

Fig. 7 a shows the high-resolution XPS Ti 2p regions of TNT and G-TNT3 samples. Both samples show two main peaks appearing around 458.5 eV and 464.2 eV, corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, with a peak separation of 5.7 eV giving evidence that Ti exists in the form of Ti4+ [49]. However for the G-TNT3 sample, the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 peaks show a slight shift toward higher binding energy, an indication of bond formation between the GQDs and TiO2 nanotubes [50]. Further, a lowered intensity of Ti peaks in sample G-TNT3 is observed, as compared to TNT samples, which suggests TNT surface passivation by GQDs. Fig. 7b displays the O 1s region of both TNT and G-TNT3 samples, depicting the main peak around 530 eV with O 1s shoulder peak appearing at 532 eV, respectively assigned to lattice and non-lattice oxygen. The O 1s peak of sample G-TNT3 shows a slight shift towards higher energy than the TNT sample, indicating possible formation of bonds between GQDs and the nanotubes [51]. The deconvolution of G-TNT3 sample’s O 1s region (Fig. 7c) exhibits three peaks consisting of a characteristic TiOTi lattice peak located at 530 eV [52], while the other two shoulder peaks appearing at 530.80 eV and 532 eV are attributed to the non-lattice TiOC and/or surface adsorbed CO groups, respectively [51]. Fig. 7d shows the raw and deconvoluted G-TNT3 C 1s region; the three peaks for C 1s located at 284.50 eV, 285.96 eV and 288.63 eV can be associated, respectively, with CC bonds due to sp2 hybridization in graphene, oxygenated carbon species such as COC, and the presence of TiOC bonds. The peak located at 288.63 eV suggests the presence of GQDs on the G-TNT3 surface, presumably due to TiOC bonds between TiO2 and GQDs [53].

The optical absorbance of the TNT and G-TNT samples was investigated by UV–vis DRS and is shown in Fig. 8 . The TNT sample shows a sharp absorption peak at 390 nm corresponding to a band gap of 3.18 eV [54,55], while exhibiting a broad absorption ‘hump’ in the visible range (400–800 nm) that can be attributed to photonic effects induced by the nanotube arrays [56–58]. The G-TNT samples show significantly enhanced absorption in the visible range, attributed to the GQDs present in the respective samples. While the amount of GQDs increases with electrodeposition time, from 1 s to 5 s, absorption increases from G-TNT1 to G-TNT3 and then decreases for G-TNT4 and G-TNT5; presumably the decrease in light absorption is due to thin film formation with loss of individual particle shape. The absorption spectra for liquid-phase as-synthesized GQDs show two absorption peaks, see Fig. S5, a characteristic typical of GQDs, centered at wavelengths of 260 nm and 350 nm respectively attributed to the π/π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain and electron excitation from ground to excited state [59].

Fig. 9 shows the transient photocurrent response, AM 1.5 illumination, light ON/OFF interval of 20 s, for TNT and G-TNT samples in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. Sample G-TNT3 shows a maximum photocurrent density of 0.3 mA cm−2, ≈1.7 times higher than TNTs. Such an abrupt increase of photocurrent density suggests improved photogenerated charge extraction and transfer due to the electrodeposited GQDs. So too, the Nyquist electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots for sample G-TNT3 (Fig. S6), which are of smaller radius than those of the TNT, suggest efficient photogenerated charge extraction and transfer due to the GQDs.

Photocatalytic MB dye degradation efficiency for TNT and G-TNT samples under AM 1.5 illumination was investigated. The rate constants for MB dye degradation were calculated using ln (Co/C) = kt, where Co represents the initial concentration of MB dye and C represents the dye concentration after reaction time (t), see Fig. 10 a [60]. The apparent rate constant (k) for the pseudo-first-order kinetic is deduced from the linear fitting of ln (Co/C) vs. t. Sample G-TNT3 exhibits maximum MB dye degradation with a k value of 0.135 h−1, over 2 times greater than TNT (0.066 h−1), see Fig. 10b. Fig. S7 shows the comparison of dye degradation rate curves for 4 h (240 min) of irradiation; G-TNT3 shows the maximum dye degradation (42.5% after 240 min of irradiation). G-TNT3 stability was tested through four sequential cycles of MB dye degradation testing, see Fig. 10c, with no diminishment seen in sample performance. Photogenerated electrons (e−) in the TiO2 conduction band are efficiently extracted by the GQDs due to its work function being more negative (ɸGQDs, −4.4 eV vs. vacuum) than the conduction band edge potential of TiO2 (−4.2 eV vs. vacuum) [61]. The GQDs can generate superoxide radical anions (O2
−) by the reduction of absorbed O2 with the photogenerated electrons extracted from the TiO2, while the holes (h+) photogenerated in the TiO2 valence band, due to their higher oxidation potential, can generate OH [62]; Fig. 10d shows the proposed MB dye degradation mechanism under AM 1.5 illumination.

The ability of the samples to promote photocatalytic conversion of CO2 and water vapor into hydrocarbon products, notably methane, under AM 1.5 illumination was investigated; a schematic of the experimental apparatus can be seen in Fig. S1. For 3 h photocatalytic exposure, Fig. 11 a shows the hour-normalized methane evaluation rate for TNT and G-TNT samples, among which G-TNT3 shows a maximum rate of methane formation of 1.98 ppm cm−2 h−1, a value 5.6 times higher than the TNT sample (0.35 ppm cm−2 h−1). The photocatalytic methane yield measured under UV light (λ = 365 nm, 1.2 mW cm−2) was 0.53 ppm cm−2 h−1, and under visible light (100 W Xenon solar simulator in combination with a λ > 430 nm cut-off filter), 0.16 ppm cm−2 h−1, see Fig. S8. As the amount of GQDs increases beyond our optimal sample the methane evaluation rate decreases, a behavior we attribute to lower light absorption and less effective charge separation, as suggested by our UV–vis DRS and transient photocurrent measurements.

Control experiments were conducted to identify the origin of carbon intrinsic to the photogenerated CH4. Sample G-TNT3 produced negligible CH4 under an Ar/H2O(g) atmosphere, indicating the carbon source in the photocatalytic reaction is from CO2. Using a G-TNT3 sample under an 13CO2/H2O(g) atmosphere, see Fig. S9, we find the GC–MS chromatogram exhibits the 13CH4 and 13CO2 peaks with an m/z value of 17 and 45, confirming CO2 as the carbon source.

Photocatalytic stability was investigated by using the same G-TNT3 sample for five sequential 3 h photocatalytic CO2 conversion test runs; as seen in Fig. 11b, the performance of the sample decreased ≈15% with each 3 h cycle. However when the G-TNT3 samples were thermo-vacuum treated (100 °C, 4 h) at the end of each 3 h run to remove surface adsorbed intermediate species [63], the photocatalytic activity of the sample was restored to its original performance, see Fig. 11c.

Referring to Fig. 1, the TiO2 (anatase) conduction band lies at −4.2 eV vs. vacuum, and the GQDs work function (ɸGQDs) at −4.4 eV [61]; the redox potential of CO2/CH4 is even more negative at −4.6 eV [64]. However, tests conducted under visible light (λ > 430 nm) indicate GQDs directly serve to promote CO2 photoreduction although, as would be expected, at quite small amounts. Overall activity is significantly enhanced in sensitization of the TiO2 nanotubes with GQDs, presumably because GQDs: (i) extend the light absorption into the visible region (Fig. 8); (ii) serve as an electron acceptor for increased charge separation (Fig. 9); and (iii) serve as a reaction center facilitating CO2 reduction (Fig. 11). Based on the commonly accepted proton assisted multi-electron process [65] required for photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to methane we suggest the underlying reactions are as follow:
(2) G-TNT + hv → G-TNT (e−) + G-TNT (h+)
(3) TNT (8h+) + 4H2O → 8H+ + 2O2
(4) CO2 + 8H+ + GQDs (8e−) → CH4 + 2H2O

In summary, a facile and unique synthesis approach, GQDs electrodeposition on a Ti-foil substrate followed by its electrochemical anodization, was designed to sensitize TNT, possessing a well-oriented and open-pore surface, with GQDs. As compared to TNT samples, the photoelectrochemical efficacy of optimized samples showed an enhanced photocurrent density, decreased impedance, and MB dye photodegradation properties, indicating that the GQDs promote efficient charge separation.

The performance of the samples were investigated for photocatalytic conversion of CO2 into CH4, with representative sample G-TNT3 exhibiting a 5.6x times greater methane formation rate. Under AM 1.5 illumination, photogenerated conduction band electrons are efficiently extracted by the GQDs and transferred to the photocatalyst surface where they can react with adsorbed CO2 molecules, as well as protons generated by water oxidation, resulting in CH4 formation. Holes photogenerated in the TiO2 valence band promote water oxidation, in turn producing protons and oxygen [64]. The improved and stable performance of the GQDs-sensitized TNT is attributed to the efficient extraction and transfer of photogenerated charges. We believe that the material design approach can be employed to help create high performance photocatalyst systems for a variety of applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the funding support by the DGIST R&D Program of the Ministry of Science and ICT(18-BD-0404) and Basic Science research program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (2017R1E1A1A01074890). Authors also acknowledge the support by the Technology Development Program to Solve Climate Changes of the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (2015M1A2A2074670). Authors are also thankful to the colleagues and friends for their kind support and help.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcou.2018.04.004.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
